The concept here is that having the right process for creating a promotion will help ensure that you have everything that you need to create a winner. It'll ensure that you're able to spend the majority of your time majoring in majors instead of looking for this piece of research, looking for that piece of research, trying to remind yourself what the offer is, trying to just figure out who your customer is, right? And make sure that you have the time to do it right. Now, let's say you're Carlene and this guy calls up and says, because you're white, he wants you to write a package. It'll normally happen something like this. There'll be some kind of an exploratory discussion. And during that discussion, the client will tell you basically what's needed. He'll describe the product to you. And you'll talk to him and tell him what you did. And if you guys come to a meeting in the mines where it looks like you might want to move ahead together, he'll offer you the project. And you'll discuss your fees. And they'll usually be a contract involved. We'll talk about that later. Then there'll be a launch call. And the launch call is typically where the editor of the service or the creator of the product meets with you on the phone along with the client's marketing people. And you discuss where we want to go with this project, whether it's a package or a sales page or whatever. On that call, it's part brainstorming and it's part you as a copywriter saying, here's what I need. Asking questions. Then you will quite often, now it's never always done this way. But this is fairly typical. Then you would be asked for a platform. A platform is usually like a headline in the first few paragraphs of sales copy. And you may have to go back and forth with the client a couple of times before you get the platform just the way you both want it. Then once you know what you're, you're going to know now what your theme is. You're going to know basically the direction that the package is going to go. So at that point, there'll be a research request. Now, I insist that my clients produce a research for me. You can't always get away with that. But when we were working at Health and Healing together, we were able to find some researchers and because they didn't have them on staff, freelance researchers and they helped us out in that regard. We'll talk about how to get researchers if you don't already have them or if your client doesn't have them. Then you'll receive back the actual research and you'll be able to produce the first draft. So we're going to look at some of the principles here that I try to always observe when I'm writing a major project. First give yourself time. The worst thing in the world for you as an earner is to have a big flop. It doesn't mean your career's over. It doesn't mean you'll never get hired again. It doesn't mean that client won't hire you again. But you just invested weeks or a month of your time to write this promotion and if it flops, that equals no revenue for you, no royalties for you as a result of all that work. So that's to be avoided at all costs. Well if you're rushing through the project because you didn't give yourself enough time, enough time to think about it, enough time to be creative, enough time to sleep on it, that's going to end up costing you a lot of money. So it's really important to make sure every project you give yourself plenty of time for the planning of the project, for the research in the project, for the writing, the actual writing work and for the editing. And when we get to the writing work, I'm going to show you how I do it. I highly recommend it. But none of this stuff is like do or die. You've got to work out your own style. But I'm going to give you some ways that work really well for me. So now let's say you've been offered the job. A really good technique is often to say, you know, let me think about it. Let me sleep on it and I'll get back to you. You know, I think I'd like to do it, but I just want to, I want to think about it. I'll make sure I'm the right guy for you or gal. Take a day, get online, research the client, okay? Look at his competitors. Look at the copy that appears most often on their websites. Now, in the short period of time, if you haven't already opted into their easing, if you're in a health niche, for example, you should be opting into every easing in the health niche. So this, you wouldn't have a situation where you don't know the client at all, right? You should have a file or you can write a rule in Outlook to have everything that guy ever sends go into a special file folder. So you can go to that file folder when you do get a job with that guy and pull out all of these emails and all of these, each email with a link that goes to a sales page. So you can go to the sales page, you can see what his copy looks like. Also, by looking at the number of emails he uses to promote a particular product, you can, in a particular campaign, you can get a real good read of what's working. Obviously, if a sales page isn't working, you're not going to see a lot of emails driving people to that sales page. But if you see 100 emails all driving the same sales page, for the same product, and you know that copy was successful, it'll take your understanding that niche farther. So and do the same thing for competitors. Really get a grasp on what's working, what kind of tone, what kind of offer, what kind of theme is working in these various areas. Prepare for the launch meeting. It's important that a launch meeting to not come off sounding like a no at all. The minute you do that, you're going to say, well, you need to do this. What's the client going to say? We tried that and it failed. So now you look like a real boob, right? So it's much better to say, hey, have you tried this? Have you tried that? Have you tried the other thing? And now you're looking really smart to the client because you're asking questions maybe about things that he's actually tried and he'll be able to tell you if they worked or not. And you need to ask every question you can think of. But here's some of the questions that you can ask on that call. What's worked best for you before? What was your most successful promotion? What was your most successful theme for this product or a similar product? What hasn't worked? What surprised you because it didn't work? Who's your prospect? How old is he or she? How much money? The average age. Is there an ethnicity question? And if the answer is yes, does it really even matter? What's the educational? A lot of these good clients will have full demographic and psychographic profiles of their customers and they'll be able to tell you who you're talking to. Which product benefit resonates best with your prospects? If the company's been around for a while, they've done some tests and they know that when people buy, and there's another way to know too, people buy a product what they're expecting from that product. And by looking at that, you can say, well, that needs to be right near the top of my letter somewhere, maybe in my deck, maybe it's my headline, I don't know. One of the cool ways of knowing what people expected when they bought the product is to ask for the complaint letters. I want to see the complaints in the emails from people who canceled or asked for a refund. I want to hear from the people who really hate this product. Because they'll say in that email, when I bought this, I bought it because you said, and I expected this, and I didn't get it. So now you know what they're expecting. And that'll inform your decisions which way you want to go. Now if the product really didn't deliver those things, then obviously you don't want to go that way. But a lot of times you'll find situations where the product did, but you got a cranky customer, it was just buyer's remorse or something like that. Testimonials, extremely important, we'll talk about this later. What you can do if the client doesn't have them. And also some important points about gathering them. Same thing with quotes from outside experts. Favorable press mentions, does he have a PR department? Are they getting mentioned in the press? If so, do you have any videos of you on CNBC? Do you have transcripts or articles you wrote for The Wall Street Journal or for Prevention Magazine? Are there any favorable third party references to the key ingredient or process? The guy says no, nobody ever says anything nice about me. You say okay fine, well I notice that there's a sawpulmetto in your product. What favorable press mentions or mentions in medical journals or other places about sawpulmetto can you find? That'll help you a lot. Can I talk to a dozen or so customers? Can I get a dozen or so samples of the product? If you can do that, you can hand it out to your friends and get from them testimonials. Also find out what they didn't like about it. It'll really help you understand your product. Interviewing customers. Now these are gonna probably be people who like the product. So you'll be getting a testimonial, but hopefully you'll also be getting a case history or a story. And stories sell better than anything. If you can tell the story of Joe who was scheduled for a heart bypass. And started taking oral chelation and went back to the cardiologist. He says you don't need the bypass anymore. And you can tell that story with some compassion and talk about what that meant to his family, what it meant to his grandkids. What it meant to his wife, what it meant to him. Man, you got him. You do two or three of those in a package and that's your whole package. How quickly do you ship? There's so many people of copywriters who forget to cover this. But adding the word instantly to an online product that you're selling. Download it instantly. Doubles response. So if it's an offline company and they ship the same day the order is received, that's a big deal. People want instant gratification. You need to know that. What can we do to enhance the value of the product? Have you been thinking about adding more of this ingredient or changing the packaging, or changing the pricing, or testing the price? What can we do to improve the guarantee? We'll talk more about guarantees in a minute. But guarantees are necessary. They work, they increase response rates. If you're dealing with smaller companies, you're gonna deal with some people who just don't like guarantees. Cuz they see it as a way that, make sure that they're gonna have to give away money at some point. They don't see the fact that by offering the guarantee, they're gonna make a lot more money in the first place. So we'll talk about some ways to do guarantees. But a surprise in the guarantee is probably one of the best things you can do in a package. Instead of just say, try this thing for 90 days and if you don't like it, we'll give you your money back. That's a typical guarantee. But if instead you have a performance guarantee tied to it, this will double your money within 90 days. Or this will solve your prostate problem within 90 days. And if it doesn't send us the empty bottle, anything that surprises or dimensionalizes what the product does in the guarantee is very helpful. What can we do to create scarcity? Part of every great offer is an urgency factor of some kind, an urgency motivator. If you buy this today, you get this discount, you get this extra gift, you get this bonus. So what can we do to create scarcity? Maybe, or urgency, maybe it's only 50 available at this price. Maybe it's only good until midnight tonight. But something that remember, what you're doing with your promotion, with your copy, is you're saying, what can I do to get this prospect to buy this product from me, not from someone else on the internet, from me today, not tomorrow. So think about urgency, what can I do to get that sale today? Okay, now it's time to do some more poking around online. And just Google everything. If it's a health product, Google every ingredient in the product. You're gonna find all kinds of stories about it. If it's investment in a particular type of investment, let's say it's world currency options, you Google that. Now you're not just seeing promotions for that for products around that product at this point. You're around that thing at this point. What you're doing is you're getting into communities of people who are interested in this, you're reading message boards. You're seeing what different people are saying about the product or this type of product. It's another way of getting inside your prospect's head. Get plenty of social proof. This is so important. Social proof is just a fancy way of saying testimonials, right? So there's several kinds of proof you want in your packages, okay? Personal proof is when you go to them and you reason with them. And you say, doesn't it just make sense that if you do this or if you take that, that this other thing will happen? That's social proof, I mean personal proof. Cuz he's proving it to himself as he reads through the copy. Social proof is where the testimonial or the third party press reference or whatever. So you can go and get more testimonials than he has by engaging the file on a blog if it's online, okay? You can ask people the key questions about the product. And not just what do you think of the product? But give me a story of how this product has helped you. And if you engage them on a blog, then you can have a two way conversation each day where you send an email to the file and you go. Today's question, the question of the day, I do this all the time, it works great. The question of the day today is, have you ever tried FX investing? FX trading, currency trading? Yes, no, okay? So the people who have tried it raise their hands. And then now you're wanting to find objections. And so you start asking them, well, why aren't you doing more of it? Was it profitable for you? Meet me on the blog, let's talk about it. And so the guys who are raising their hands or having this conversation with you, a lot of that material, if you have the right disclaimer copy on the blog site is usable for testimonials. Do a survey, we do that a lot. We usually attach some kind of a gift for their answer. Of course as copy writers, we would like to only give the gift for the helpful answers. The people that say, we love you. But you have to give gifts to anybody. And the gift can be a special report, can be a special online event with a celebrity or with the head of the company. It can be really anything you want it to be. On one survey where we really wanted to get the file going, we actually turned it into a contest. And there were some legalities involved with it that I don't really remember now. But the top prize was a trip to Hawaii. So, a survey, any of you ever use SurveyMonkey online? Okay, it's a real easy way, even I can create a survey on SurveyMonkey. And you just write your questions and then you grab the link and you put it in your emails and you drive people to SurveyMonkey. And you can have questions that are multiple choice or you can have questions that are just like open field questions. Right? So here's some stuff to know about testimonials. You need to have a release on the page where they're giving you the testimonial, whether it's a survey page or a blog page or whatever. And that essentially says, I grant you permission to use this. Use what I say. Now your client will no doubt have a legal beagle or compliance officer or somebody on staff that'll write that language. But there needs to be a release. It's a shame if you go through this entire process to get some great testimonials and you can't legally use them. Our compliance officers require that the person is still a customer. So a testimonial that we got two years ago from a newsletter subscriber who's no longer subscribed and who obviously no longer loves the product is not usable. So keep that in mind. Disclaimers, results not typical. I'd strongly recommend that when you guys have a little bit of spare time that you go to the Federal Trade Commission site. I have the URL there on the slide. And they have a section that's all for advertisers. And it'll give you real good guidance on what you can and can't do in terms of testimonials in your copy and the disclaimers that need to go along with them. All right, the next step I take is to, I just sit down and think, I know my customer now. I've asked all the questions I need to understand the customer. By the way, I just want to throw this out there. And if you're doing direct mail especially, if you want to know more about your customer and your prospect and your client is unwilling or unable to give you more information, go down to the library and grab a copy of the standard rate data service and look up his company in that. If he's not in it, then his competitors will be, most likely. And that will give you psychographic information. The kinds of products this guy has bought before, the kinds of other products he reads or the media that he reads, where they've been advertising to get this customer. And demographics, I'll tell you the age, sex, all that stuff about who you're talking to. And there's an SRDS for email lists as well as direct mail lists. So you want those two questions answered to really round out your mental picture of who you're talking to, demographics, psychographics. Now you can quite often do this just intuitively like Carleen did. She says, my mom doesn't know anything about vitamins. I think I'll write her a letter, and that works because her mom was her target market. So she was the right age and she was, I don't know what other demographics might have fit, but great way to go. So some other ways to get to know your prospect. Buy and read magazines and books that are designed for his market, okay, for people like him. Is a modern maturity, is a trailer life, is it guitar god magazine, what is it? What is he reading besides your promotion? You can hunt relevant websites and blogs. I love blogs because there's no hype. It's just people like you and me just talking. And if there's something they like or something they don't like, they'll be very candid about it. I love cable TV because with 350 channels and we're all specializing now in one area or another. There's good housekeeping TV, there's speed TV for people who like cars. There's financial TV channels for people like investments. There's health channels for people who are interested in that. So start watching those. What you're trying to do here again is get inside your prospect's head. What we're doing is the most important thing we can do is to understand what he wants, what he fears, what he frustrates him. And cultivate friendships with the likely prospects. Put your money where your mouth is. And what that means is go to the websites where your people go, where your prospects go, and buy the products. Get on those people's lists, okay? So you can see more of their advertising. But then also go to the bookstore and have a budget of 100 bucks or 200 bucks. And buy magazines and books that relate back to that. I used to do this and be able to actually steal subheads right out of the tables of contents of certain books that were written in my market. So at this point, you're really inside the head of your prospect. One other thing is kind of like the method acting thing. Where you just basically say, how would I feel if I was 65 years old and had arthritis? How would I feel? And close your eyes and just think about that. And kind of feel your way through the process of listening to your doctor that you have heart disease. Say that you have heart disease. So if you do that, that'll take you a long way. Yes, Karleen, you wanna grab the mic? I was gonna say to add to what you're saying, if you're not at your market age. I mean, I started, I was in my early 30s. And I really couldn't relate a lot to my market. So what I used to do is literally, I'd have a bunch of my friends come over. And if I was writing about a cancer package, for example, I would call and invite my friends who are 40s, 50s, 60s. And who maybe had issues with cancer or cancer scares. Would they like mostly from church? Whoever was my friend. Okay, all right. It's a challenge to be my friend because I use them all the time. If you look at my promotions, you'll find my family and my friends' testimonials because I would go, I need you to try this and let me know how it works because it's a new launch or something. So yeah, from church, from my neighbors, whoever. As long as they were the target market that I needed, I'd invite them over to my house, I'd have dinner there, and I'd have like a round circle discussion and just say, listen, I'm working on this project and I just wanna know what's going on. I wanna hear it because when you're talking to them one on one, you're hearing their words and you just take what they're saying and incorporate that into your copy because I wasn't my market. Now I'm in my market, so I don't have to have my- I always thought that was brilliant. I remember those parties you were having. You learned some stuff. It was great. I mean, I give them a gift for coming or whatever, thank them. But we just have a good time. It'll be maybe eight to ten people. It was harder with the men, invited them to come over and talk about their prostate. But I would do it. It was just, hey, I need to understand who I'm writing to because it makes no sense to start writing if you don't know who you're talking to. Absolutely. You know what, Paris takes it one step further and when you finish that, what he has to do is- Oh, right. I didn't even talk about that. Paris has an exercise that he does with us. He takes it one step further. So after you meet with them, you have to go home and write in their voice, like a journal entry, like, oh my God, I got up this morning and my knees were killing me. I really wanted to go hang out with my grandkids, but I could, like, you have to then channel them and take some of the details that you got and you write like you wrote their journal entry for a page or so. Beautiful. Now, one thing I want to point out here, you notice when all of these other copywriters started jumping in here and talking about this and slowing me down? This is important. This is what successful copywriters do. How many of you were on the webinar a week or so ago where I spanked everybody for their letters? What was the unforgivable sin that was committed in writing those letters? Didn't know who your audience was. Your first thought was not, who am I talking to? So that's how important this is. You've got Marcella all excited and you've got Carly and all excited. I can see David's back there and Champin at the bit say something too. Anyway, very important to crawl inside your prospects head. The next thing, now you've got your prospects squared away. You know who you're talking to. You know his fears, frustrations, desires, you know what he's responded to in the past. Right? Next step is the product. Now you look at the product. What does it do? What are the benefits? If I buy this, how's it going to make my life better? And we're going to talk more about benefits in a minute. But there's a whole hierarchy of benefits on a product. And I'm not going to, yeah, I'm so tempted to go there now, but I'm not going to. But anyway, think though about the practical benefit. If I buy this investment course, it's going to make me richer. Okay, fine, that's good benefit. But what are the emotional benefits? What will it do for him emotionally? Will it make people respect him more? Will it give him peace of mind? Will it help him relax about his security and retirement? And what are the emotional benefits? I said this a million times, you've probably heard it a million times, but nobody ever needs anything you're selling. They don't. They'd probably be better off putting the money away in their retirement kitty. Bottom line is, and this is not original with me, I'm quoting an addict, a woman, I forget her name. But all you ever need is a cave of fire and a recently deceased squirrel. That's all you really need. So the things that we buy are not out of necessity. They're out of an emotional need of some kind. We want status, we want respect, we want to love, we want peace of mind, some emotional need that we have. So identify the emotional needs that the product addresses. Now, you have your product and you have your product. Now it's time to kind of look outside and say what's happening? What's the weather like in this area? If it's an investment area, the question is, what's in the headlines now? What is in the headlines now that's likely to still be there in a year? And what you're looking, and in the health area, what's going on there? There's some new discovery. There's some new study that's been all over the media about inflammation in your arteries and how Lipitor can reduce it. Okay, some theme that your customer, your potential customer, is reading about, thinking about, and he'll then have questions about. So if you can choose something like that as your lead theme, you're gonna stop him, he's gonna read or he's gonna watch your video. So the question here is, what can I do or say to stop my prospect in his tracks? And make reading or listening to this message the most important thing he could do today. And it's really important to think about it that way. I've envisioned my prospect in his kitchen with my direct mail piece in front of him. He's about to pick it up and the kids put the cat in the microwave. Meanwhile, the phone rings, the doorbell rings, all hell breaks loose in the house. My goal is to make him still read my piece and let the cat fry. So have that image in your mind. What can I do to make reading this the single most important thing my prospect could do? And part of that, it's very important if you look at anything I've written or Carlene's written or Paris or David, Marcella, any of us, you're gonna see that there's a readership bribe right up front. There's something that says, in this report, I'm gonna teach you this or I'm gonna give you that or I'm gonna name this. I'm gonna name the five best tech stocks today. I'm gonna name 50 stocks you should sell immediately. The value added information in the report is the bribe that you're paying your reader for reading it. And having that right up front is very important. One of the things I'm gonna suggest later is, of course, reading the masters every year, John Capels is my favorite of that group. And hold it up. This is what his book looks like. It's a very quick read, but if you do it right, it could take months to get through it. One of the things Capels does is he gives you great lists of ways he evaluates copy or way he evaluates offers, okay? Tested advertising methods is the book, as it pays is also great. So now you're, again, we're on theme, developing the theme for your package, right? So here's some of the themes that you might think of as you're looking at your product in your market. Make more money, save money, you can read them, they're in your book, right? Is this in your book? Okay, all right, make more money, save money. And save money can be applied, it should be applied to every product you ever sell. You know, there should be an offer with a discount of some kind as an incentive for buying today. Retirement security, that could be health security, could be financial security, could be something else. Better health immediately. Solving a health problem right now today is far more valuable to your reader than preventing one in the future. So if you're tempted to go with a lead theme that's about making sure that you don't have a heart attack sometime in the future, you need to think harder about it. And see if there's something that you can do to bring that into the present. One way that I've done it is to basically say everyone has heart disease. I wrote a package once, everyone has cancer all the time. The question is, can your body stop what's happening that's detrimental to you? And if the answer is yes, but you have to take this one thing, you've got people. You brought it into the present. Advancing a profession or trade, that's B2B right there really, I think. Prestige, now prestige is really an emotional benefit. And that makes a lot of sense to me, it would be on here. Enjoyment, leisure time, easier chores, gain more leisure, comfort, reduce fat, freedom from worry, another emotional benefit. So when you read capals, you'll have the opportunity to make yourself dozens of checklists, dozens of checklists that you can use. And then when you write the copy, you can take that checklist and after having read your copy, you can just check off the things that you've done and circle the things that you haven't and go back for one of your editing passes and address those things. That'll take your copy farther, faster than just about anything that you can do. Now you know the product benefits and you know the emotions and desires of your prospect, you just need to connect them, right? You need to put them together some way in your deck copy or in your opening copy especially. And then write two or three mantras. Now a mantra is different from a USP. A unique selling proposition is something about your product that's absolutely unique, you can't get anywhere else. And it's the reason why he should buy your product. A mantra is really a statement that you can repeat over and over again throughout the copy that will drive a key benefit home. So here are some examples. Campaign that I'm in the middle of right now, the mantra is tech stocks, it's time to dream big again. We really liked that, I didn't write it. The editor, the service actually wrote it, but I really liked it because I immediately felt that surge of excitement that I had back in the 90s watching my stocks just shoot the moon every day, you know? So it connected me with that. Safe and sane investing in a world gone mad. This would be for a safe money report, one of my newsletters that focuses on safer investing. I use this when Sir John Templeton's most guarded wealth building secret made six and a half million dollars with that, this time last year. Cancel your appointment in the emergency room. Now that's a good emotional mantra, right? Now it doesn't use hyperbole, it doesn't use big strong superlative words, it just scares the crap out of you. I don't remember, Carling, which product it was, but we had the cover test with a gun pointed right at the reader. Prescription roulette? Prescription roulette, yeah. Yeah, yeah. Now there were no exclamation points in that, there didn't have to be. The visual on that one really carried the day. And your next heart attack doesn't have to happen. I wrote a package for health resources that in different permutations meld about 60 million pieces. And this was the mantra, your next heart attack doesn't have to happen. So again, different from the USP, but it's like an anchor point that you come back to over and over again, a theme that you come back to over and over again in your copy. Very powerful technique. So basically now, at this point in your process, you are equipped to begin writing. And well, almost, almost. At this point, you have, you've dug in your product, you need to identify all of the benefits, pull them out. I have an extra sheet on my desk or a document on my desktop that basically has all of this available to me that I can go back to at any time. Identify every positive emotion, identify every negative issue that the product resolves. Every negative emotion your product addresses. Add premiums to meet major needs not met by the product itself or just to add value. Now in the newsletter world, this is important if you haven't written for newsletters before. In the newsletter world, if you give the benefits of subscribing to Safe Money Report, for example, right? It's gonna be, you'll invest safer. I can't tell you what's gonna be in the next issue, so I can't really be a lot more specific than that. So the tried and true approach for writing financial newsletter promotions, or any newsletter promotion for that matter, is to basically sell the premium, sell the free gift that the person gets when they subscribe to the newsletter. So your theme and everything is built into that free report that they're gonna get if they sign up. Now you can tell them specifically what's in the report. On page such and such, you'll get this. On page such and such, you'll get that. And so it's very, very clear. And then search for a way to position your product at the top of its own ladder in prospects minds. If you haven't read positioning, you need to get a copy of it and read it. It helped me make a lot of money years ago, back in the 80s. But the idea there, and I did this at my power marketing summit, but how many of you can name the first guy's step foot on the moon? Okay, how many can name the second? Point taken, right? So getting your product under the top rung of a ladder in your prospect mind is key. And the way to do that is to position it in a way so that it's unique and different from every other product in this area. I don't have time to go into this in detail. You get the basic idea, get the book positioning. It should really be a major part of your marketing arsenal. I'm a big one for this, and this is a step that a lot of writers don't do. I ask myself, what does my prospect have to believe? Really believe in order to make buying this product a no-brainer. Okay, so let's say you're selling a service on gold. That tells people how to invest in gold. What does a prospect have to believe in order to want that product? Well, he would first have to believe that gold is gonna go up. Most likely he would have to believe that gold is gonna go up because the Federal Reserve has been printing money like it's going out of style. And printing more money than it did in the 70s when we had 18% inflation and 21% interest rates. So if my prospect has to believe those things in order to want a service on gold, well, another thing he's gonna have to believe that my newsletter, my analyst, is gonna help him invest in gold in a way that's gonna be highly profitable for him. So what am I gonna need? I'm gonna need a track record from my editor that proves that this guy knows what he's doing and he's been making people money. I'm gonna need facts and figures about the last time gold prices took off. They went from $35 to $850 an ounce back in the 70s, in early 80s. So I'm gonna need details about all of that. And I gather the information I'm gonna need to make that point, to make sure that my prospect believes that. Once he believes those two things, that gold's about to explode and my editor is the best guy in the universe to help him make money with rising gold. He'd be insane not to buy my service. So to the degree that I'm able to make those two points and make him believe those things, I'm gonna win. So now you've identified those things and now what do you do? What kind of proof elements am I gonna need to make those points? Okay, you gotta find them. And I start out by saying, what is just my dream proof element on a particular thing? It might be a quote from Warren Buffett. It might be a quote from the Surgeon General or Linus Pauling. We've used Linus Pauling a lot in the past for health products. It might be T-Boon Pickens standing up and saying that natural gas prices are about to crash. And as a result, there's a huge profit opportunity on the short side for investors. Huge authority. Okay, well, what if I can't get that? I do a lot of video sales pages, so my dream would be to have T-Boon Pickens sitting in the studio with my client telling him that you're crazy if you don't invest in oil right now. So what if I can't get that? Well, then what are my backups? What other proof elements would really help me make this point? Next, create your outline so often. Now, you may follow your outline just word for word on other projects. It may just be a way of getting your mind into the promotion. I'm famous for taking my outline and then just hanging the research on each point. Okay, now I never have to start with an empty page. I've got an outline. I know basically the points I have to make to sell this product. And I've hung the research on each point so I can prove each point beyond a shadow of a doubt. Now it's simply a matter of turning that outline into a conversation. Makes writing very easy. And you go from outline to first draft in a matter of hours or days depending on the size of the project. So pretty good starter outline. Restate the headline proposition, assuming you have a headline to start with hopefully. Don't want one of those headless wonders. Restate the headline proposition theme with some detail. Introduce yourself, give your qualifications. And when you restate the headline proposition, you could say, yes, I said the Dow's going to 41,000. I know you think I'm nuts. My wife thinks I'm certifiably insane. But please, hear me out. Because if I'm right, you could make more money in less time than you ever have in your entire life. Wow, okay? So what do you do next? Well, if I'm listening to what you just said, my next question is, and this is how you lead yourself through the copy, right? So what's my next question? Who the hell are you? Anybody can say stuff like that. Why should I listen to what you're saying? My name's Clayton Makepeace, and by the way, here's my track record. I predicted this crash and that crash and this boom and that boom. And the last five investment recommendations I made jumped this much, respectively. So you're presenting your credentials, credentializing the speaker, right? Now you tell the story. You're gonna have to explain to me why the Dow is going to 41,000, okay? Or why I'll never have a heart attack or whatever your theme is. So now you tell your story. And like I said earlier, if you tell that story using people's stories, that's the most powerful way to do it. We did one on inflation and we were able to find three or four stories of people who went through massive inflation in Brazil and in Russia. And then we were able to go back in time and get human interest stories about people who went through the inflation of the Weimar Republic in Germany. And what it meant to them, you know? Otherwise, inflation's just a word. People are starting to think, okay, so I'll pay 20 cents more a gallon, you know? But no, that's not what it is. It's basically taking a wheelbarrow full of dollar bills down to the bakery to buy a loaf of bread. And the guy says, I don't want the dollar bills, but I'll take the wheelbarrow. You know? Cuz it has some value. So, you know, what you're looking for is a way to tell your story in the most entertaining way possible. We talk about infotainment, that's basically what we do. And so make sure you don't forget the attainment. We're gonna talk more about that later. Present your solution. It may be a special report connected to the subject if you're selling a newsletter. It may be the product itself if you're selling a three dimensional product of some kind. Now it's time to extol all of the benefits of the product, the practical benefits and the emotional ones. Present your offer. Trivialize the price. You know, this is sofa cushion, money. A cup of Starbucks costs more than this. You know, a cup of coffee a day costs more than this. It's less than a gallon of gas or a tank of gas depending on your price. Trivialize the price, just, you know, scoff at it. Put it up there and just make fun of it, you know? Eliminate risk, that's your guarantee. Present your urgency motivator. Okay, at this point, if you've done your job, this guy is absolutely sold on buying this product from you today. Okay, so now you want him to do it, not later today, you want to do it immediately. So what are you going to do to do that? And we talked about it a little bit earlier. You can have a time limit. You can have a number of products available at this price limit. You can have, let's see, what else could you have? I had two or three others this morning. But anyway, you get the idea. These are the reasons why you need to respond immediately. Then you restate the headline proposition. A lot of writers make the mistake of getting the big headline, the big deck, and then having the opening copy all working beautifully. And then they just kind of forget it. And they go off in the direction they go off and continually returning to that main theme, okay? Is going to make your copy much more powerful. Then finally, you ask for the sale. Now I like to ask for the sale, restate the benefits, ask for the sale again. And the final time I restate the benefits, I typically will do it in very short copy, just bullets. It'll do this, it'll do that. You'll feel this way, you'll feel that way. Life will never be the same. You save this much by buying now. You'll get this freebie if you buy now. You're an intelligent human being, you will make the right decision. That close has made me a lot of money. The idea basically, you know your needs better than I do, you'll make the right decision. Okay, the research process is very important. You've created your outline now, you look at each of those points, you say, what in the world do I need in order to make each of these points just absolutely undeniable? So again, I insist that my clients provide me with researchers. And the reason for that, when they hear the reason, even if they don't have a researcher for me, when they hear my reason for it, they'd normally say yeah. In fact, one guy said, tell you what, you go find a researcher and I'll pay him. And my reason is very simply, I'm a great copywriter and a crappy researcher. And especially, and so if you're retaining me, you don't want me researching, you want me writing copy, that's where I have the greatest value to you. Also, of course, I have the advantage of being in the financial field and to get a lot of the data I need, you need a Bloomberg term along your desk, right? That costs 2000 a month or something. And so I'm not going to buy one of those. And so you guys are going to have to provide me with this research. And now, if you can't do that, what do you do? Well, there's, if the client isn't willing to provide it, you can go to odesk, elance, fiverr.com, warrior forum, or maybe use local college students. Fiverr.com is just basically, I'll pay $5 for this. But you can really offer anything you want. So all of these sites give you access to people who do research work. Yeah? Also, AWI has a course of researchers. Yeah. So they've been trained in that area so you can tap into them and everything. Great, great. So can they just call Denise and get hooked up? All right. So wow, what did that do for you? Now instead of doing all that research, you can spend that time thinking about your project. Or jumping into the copy and starting to write. We found local college students at Weiss, we're using several local students to help out with this kind of stuff. And college kids too, on spring break and in the summer. They love this kind of work. Then drop the research in the proper places in your headline. We talked about, I mean in your outline. Now, mostly what happens when a copywriter writes a package or a promotion of some kind. He goes, okay, here's my promotion. And I've got all the way through it from beginning to end. And now I'm gonna get up tomorrow morning and I'm gonna start editing. But the question is, what are you gonna do? Are you just gonna sit there and read through it and say, that doesn't sound exactly right. Let me change that word. Or this is not really the right order, let me move this section up here. Are you gonna do it just intuitively that way? Well, if I do it that way, I'm gonna miss a lot of stuff. So I like to have an editing plan. And the plan is different depending on where the copy is, when it's basically complete. It's complete, it's not done, okay? I've got everything from the headline to the close. So I need to have a plan to make sure that I take time to consider each of these things. Is the organizational right? Is it linear? Okay, does it start at point A and go to point Z? Or does it keep looping around on itself? You need to be able to make a point and move on, make a point and move on. And just let the accumulated weight of that evidence make your sale for you. Every time you go back to a point that you made earlier and try to reprove it, what's happening? You're making the prospect feel like he's not moving through the copy. And he's thinking, I thought we established that. You had me. So it kills momentum in getting through your copy. Readability, I'm big on the five letter word rule and five lines per paragraph rule. And also on the 40 characters per line rule. A lot of things I look at, now this is formatting rather than just copy. But it was both. But I'm gonna be looking for the simplest word, the shortest word that precisely expresses what I want to express in a particular place. When I see young writers work, I can always spot it. When the copy feels diffuse, it feels like they never make a really crisp, sharp point. So going through the copy and looking at the language and asking are my word choices correct is a big key to me. So really those two points kind of come together, don't they? Readability is format, make sure your paragraphs are so short that looking at the page is not discouraging to the prospect. And make sure that the line length is short enough so that the eye doesn't fatigue moving all the way across this long line of copy. Lori's nodding her head, yeah. And then make sure your word choices are as precise as possible. There are all kinds of great quotes from great writers about the precision of great writing and it really is because if your word choices are precise, you're gonna use fewer words. And that makes the reading much easier for the prospect. So it'll stay with you longer, so your response rate will go up. Clarity plays into that. Credibility, anytime your bullshit detector starts going off, stop. You go, I don't really believe that. You get that feeling in your gut. For me, it's almost a feeling of panic. Maybe they won't notice this. I think, was it Ogilvy who said your prospect is not an idiot? She is your wife. You know, respect the intelligence of your readers, okay? Anytime you get that feeling, stop and say what can I do to make the, sometimes I'll delete the point. If I can't prove it beyond a shadow of doubt, I'll just get rid of it. But the best way to go with it, if it's a good solid selling point, would be to find another piece of research that backs it up. And then finally, go through and just ask yourself, am I being persuaded? That's the point of the exercise. If you've done all of these other things well, if it's organized well, it's readable, word choices are sharp, very clarity of thought, clarity of vision. I could do a whole day on clarity of vision, but the idea there too is, Guy will come to you and you'll say, I want to do a package on a particular theme. Maybe the theme is stock market investing. And I want to make the point that the stock market's about to crash and it'll be down for about three months and then it's going to take off like a scalded cat. No, there's no clarity of vision there. Basically what you're saying is the stock market's going to fluctuate. Who doesn't know that, right? So there's no clarity whether you're a bull or a bear. There's no clarity whether I should buy or sell. It stinks, right? And quite often you'll get those kinds of assignments you need to be able to spot them. It's always a keep it simple, stupid business. It's always the message needs to be of a single mind and very simple, clarity of vision. And credibility, we know how important that is, especially on the internet these days and persuasion. Get free reviews. I do this at every boot camp. I do this at every time I talk. But you are meeting some of the best people you will ever meet in your life right now in this room. And you can talk to other copywriters here, exchange cards, and agree to review each other's work. Paris and Marcella and David have this vast network of, I don't know how many copywriters that they work with. Now they're not doing it for this very reason. But what they end up with is this large group of writers who can all look at the copy and tell you if it does these things or not. And so take advantage of this while you're here, exchange cards with people, form your own little copywriting cells. And it'll really, really, really help you. Okay, so. I just think that's so huge. I think that's one of the things. Get him on mic. He's about to say how brilliant I am. That is just, is it on? Yeah, that is just so huge. I just think people don't do that enough. I do crits for people sometimes. And if they just would have shown it to their wife. Yeah. And said, does this make you want to buy this? Or do you understand this? How do you feel after reading this? It just, they wouldn't need me. Dan Rosenthal is. Dan Rosenthal would show it to this. The janitor was walking by. Yeah. He would be like, here, read this. Yeah, he'd walk up to a person on the street, total stranger. Literally. And shove it in their face. No exaggeration. Yeah. And if he didn't understand it, then damn it, he would make it so the janitor could understand it. Yep, yep, absolutely. One of the people that David and I use all the time is Cassandra. We met at an AWAI event. And she works with Lori as a designer. And we're sitting next to each other. And at the time I was writing with Lois and I, we're all writing for Schaefer's investment research and some other options products. And she's sitting next to me and she says, I can't even imitate her Southern accent. But she's like, now, y'all work for Schaefer's, right? And she's this little bitty, blonde-haired, beautiful little Southern girl. And I'm like, yeah. And you design with Lori. Yeah. And she says, and I trade options. And I said, what? She says, yeah, every morning I trade options. Turns out she is a cutthroat option trader. And before she gets up and does her design, she like, you know, goes out and makes a bunch of option trades in the market. And so when we did our Motley Fool campaign, we asked her to read through the copy. And she is like, because she is the audience, she'd be like, I don't believe you. This is bull crap. I mean, she would like circle, she's brutal. But because she's trading options all the time. If I get anything wrong about it, she's on it, you know, in a second. And so, and that was just sitting next to each other. So everybody here will have something that they are passionate about, that they know, and that they do. And if you are writing for that market, then now all of a sudden you've got the perfect home run. Because not only could she tell me when I was wrong about options, but she knows enough about copy to say, maybe you could, you know, do this. I remember one of her things was she said, I always thought Bernie was like 25 because you guys didn't tell me that he started trading options when they were little ads in the back of the Wall Street Journal. So then I was like, okay, somehow he's coming across too young. We're not credentializing him in time. We're just, you know, so just that little thing makes all the difference. So somebody here will know about your product that you're working on right now. Very good. I cover this later because we have another session. And remember what we're doing here is looking at the six things that can rocket your career. And of course, producing bigger winners more often is the number one item, right? So that's what we've just spent almost what an hour talking about. And we're going to cover all of the other aspects as well. But tomorrow morning we have the second part of this talk, which is going to be about the same kind of stuff. It won't be about process, but it'll be about just basically writing stronger copy. I had the situation. I was at Blanchard and Company. Every once in a while in my career, I would take on a client exclusively. You know, and the client would offer me ungodly amounts of money to not take on any other clients. And so during that period of time, I'm not a freelancer anymore. I'm not out there. I'm not talking to all of these other direct response companies. And so after five years or so of that, they kind of forget who you are. Cuz I'm not calling them, they don't know what happened to me. And so I was working with Blanchard and Company, which was a golden silver dealer in New Orleans. And we exploded the company, we quadrupled their sales, and they were like 30 million and we took them to 125 million a year in revenues. And a subsidiary of General Electric came to us and wanted to buy the company. And actually, they had me write a video. We did a half hour video on what a great company we were and then we sent it out to people who bought companies and General Electric raised their hand. And so they came to us, they bought the company. And I hated the suits. The people that they sent down to run that company couldn't find their butt with both hands. I mean, these people, they work for a company that makes locomotives for crying out loud. And now they're trying to tell us how to run a direct response where coin company. And it was disastrous. So I quit and after delivering some very serious warnings to the CEO, I said, you're gonna go out of business if you keep doing this. And they filed for bankruptcy three months later. But so now I'm faced with a problem that I had this very lucrative income from Blanchard and it was gone. And what am I gonna do now? And so these people haven't heard of me for five years. Probably the people running these companies don't even know what a Clayton Makepiece is. And so I called Bob King at Phillips. Now Phillips was a holy grail in those days. That was the company everybody wanted to work for. Because they had a great financial division. And Gary Ben-Savango was their marquee writer. I don't know if Jim Rutson started writing for him yet or not. He had. And so they were just like a dream client. Plus they paid royalties, which was not that common in those days. Mostly clients were paying a flat fee. So I called up. I said, Bob, he took my call. I said, my name is Clayton Makepiece. And I have been writing for Blanchard and Company. And Bob goes, I wondered who that was. Your stuff is awesome. And I said, well, thanks. I didn't know what to say. I was completely taken aback. This was a company that wouldn't even have talked to me five years previously. So long story short, Bob said, I'm going to make sure you get a whole bunch of assignments. And I was off to the races as a freelancer again. Then of course, the first thing I did when I had a control for Bob and for Phillips was I called Agora. And I called Rodale. And I called Niden Gelkonen and KCI, all of our competitors. And next thing you know, I was double booked. And I made a mistake. I want to tell you about my mistake. Brian Kurtz at boardroom came to me and he goes, we have a product that's basically dead. We've been promoting the heck out of it now for two or three years. It's been very successful for us. It's called The Big Black Book. But it's dead. We've thrown every writer we have at it. We can't get it to work. And I'd never written for boardroom before. But Brian knew that I was writing magilogues. Do you all know what a magilogue is? It's for those of you who didn't nod. It looks like a magazine. It's about 24 to 32 pages long. But it's really a promotion piece. So it provides a tremendous amount of information on the front end. It's an infomercial, basically, in print. Provides a tremendous amount of value added information on the front end, then offers a problem to address whatever the situation is that you're talking about. So I wrote a promotion for The Big Black Book. And it was the first magilogue that Brian had ever done, that boardroom had ever done. And it blew the doors off everything else he were doing. And I got this phone call, to all your packages work like this. I said, well, what do you mean? Nobody told me how well it's working. But it basically saved the product. And they were able to promote that product for several years thereafter. So at this point, I'm going, boardroom copy is easy, man. All you have to do is you read the book that you have to sell. And you identify the information in the book that is not a forehead slapper. It's not wow. And you turn that into a bullet. And then you identify the other information in the book that is a wow. Like, I didn't know that. I didn't know that taking my wallet out of my pocket could cure my aching back, which was one of David's. And I didn't know that. Well, that's a sidebar. So I'm going to give away the wow information. I'm going to tease the heck out of the information that isn't wow, but I really want to know. So I've got my formula for writing a great boardroom package. So I think I called Paris. And my idea, I called John Finn first, and I said, I want to be double-booked, or maybe even triple-booked. And I want to bring in other copywriters. And I want to copy-chief them to write these packages using my formula that just blew everyone away on Big Black Book. And so he did that. So I remember I sent a letter out to Brian at boardroom and all my other clients. And I just said, this is right after this big success, right? And I said in the letter, it was like March or something. And so I said, I'm setting up my bookings for the second half of the year. And I've sent this to nine major publishers. And it's my times available on a first come, first serve basis. Brian called me up and took every slot. Like $200,000 in advances. You remember that, Wendy? Took every single slot. And so now of my normal slots. Now, Brian doesn't know I'm double and triple-booking. But I eventually broke down and told him because I wanted him to take more slots. So then I called my friends who were really good writers, like Paris and a couple others. Can't remember who all at this point. But I was, at any one time, I had three packages going for boardroom. Not a single one of them worked. Not a single one of them worked. So after, and of course, it takes like three, four months to go through the process, just the first ones. But I had booked up six months. So at the end of that period of time, by then, I told Brian what I was up to. I had just tripled my income by triple-booking. Well, not exactly. Because I did pay the copyright or something. But I called him up and I said, this isn't working. I can't do this anymore. It's ruining my reputation. It's ruining our relationship. And so we just stopped doing it. But don't assume that. Now, it takes a special kind of mentality to be able to copy chief other writers. And I don't have it. I got to tell you, I don't have it. I hate it. I hate it. Marcella's great at it. David's great at it. Paris is great at it. I suck at it. Because if it's not my own copy, I find myself getting bored while I'm reading it, or frustrated. And so I remember one time Carleen turned in a pat. We have some extra time, so we're just tap dancing here. But Carleen wrote this package. And it was a sex package for men. I don't know how she always ended up with these sex packages. And she sent me the first draft. And it was lame. She was trying to be too nice. And I said, Carleen, you're talking to guys here. So I want you to hang up this phone. Remember this? I want you to burp, pass gas, adjust your package, and write it again. And she did, and it was great. That was my first package. That was a sex policy package I had gotten. Because I was a black copywriter. And that was the intro that was required to say, what do you think about it? And he's like, no, it's like you're a fucking girl. Yeah, you're right. And she goes, but I am a girl. So OK, any other questions about my process, about how I approach a package, a promotion? Yes? I have a question. Would you do a microphone? Microphone. I have a question about your outline. Yes. I'll go back a few slides. So I'm going to take the headline proposition, introduce yourself, and then tell a story. And I wonder if you would you ever recommend launching into a story right away to captivate attention, or would you always do that a step away? Yeah, that's a very valid way to go. No, it's a very valid. But it depends on the theme that you've come up for your package. So if you want to start with a story, very powerful approach, very powerful technique. So long as the story is strong. Is it Carlton that wrote how a bald-headed barber saved my hair? I think it was. It's very old. It's very old. It's very old. Yeah. But well, it's like they laughed when I sat down to play the piano. OK, that's really old. And it's a valid technique. It works very well. Yes, Chris. I just didn't want to ask first. But anyway, just further to this question, Clayton, when you start with a story in the actual lead, it's just enough to get them hooked. You would then probably go to the introduction and the qualifications. And then you're back to the story with proof, right? Yes. You always want it. You're absolutely right, Chris. You always want to introduce your speaker. You don't want to have somebody having a conversation with a nameless, faceless individual. And you always need to present the credentials of that speaker. So you can start out. I mean, let's face it. You can start out with a headline that says, buy this product. Buy this product. It does this, the big promise kind of headline. You can start out with anything that you want. But at some point in the very early going, usually for me, when I was writing a lot of direct mail for financial publications, that introduction of the speaker and presentation of credentials happened within the first two pages. Usually around the middle of the second page. But you're absolutely right. And if I may, well. OK. There was at boot camp this year, I think it was boot camp. I did the presentation on 37 different headline ideas. You can look at that. And that will show you all of the different ways in that you can have on the front end. And a story is certainly a great one. Yes? When you work with a headline, I mean, with an outline like this, sometimes it could get a little formulaic. I'm assuming that as you're looking at the demographics, as you're looking at your prospect and your product, those are the things that make it not feel like that. But if you're writing to the same demographics and the same kind of product, how do you avoid having it? Well, there's two parts to that. The first part is our headline here is pretty darn good starter outline. I should probably underline that. This is a great place to start. So you don't have to start from scratch and say, OK, well, what's my unique outline going to be for this package? You can start with this and say, OK, well, this is pretty good. It accomplishes everything I'm going to need to accomplish in the package. The second thing is, as you do your research, the product that you're researching, the market that you're researching, the prospects, are going to change this outline for you. The third thing, there's always do this when he makes fun of me. I have two answers to that. And I'll have three. But the third way to think about it is this. If your theme is different, if your product is different, if your benefits are different, this outline will be indistinguishable to the casual observer. I'll have no idea that you followed this general outline. Having said that, though, you need to kind of be, let's say you decide to go with this. And at some point, it feels like one of these things is fitting a square peg into a round hole. You have all the freedom in the world to change it. I say in tomorrow's presentation, basically, let the project tell you how it wants to get done. You may need two or three sets of credibility statements. You may not need a story at all. You may just need to do a straight product pitch. So yeah, just let the project tell you how it wants to get done. But again, go back to that 37 headline starter ideas that I did at Bootcamp. And there's a whole lot of ideas there about different ways in. But this is quite literally the outline that I used for 40 years that worked well for me. I know with me right now with writing what I kind of struggle with based on the outline is you get everything together. But as you're writing, you have to keep in mind your audience, but also the voice of the person that you're supposed to be writing for. So are there any tricks to help kind of keep everything? Is the word symbiosis or? So you know, working? Symbiotic. Symbiotic. There you go. So you have that flow through there, but keeping all those elements together? Unless my client, unless my speaker, is somebody very famous that people are familiar with their voice, I never consider voice. I never do. Now obviously, that has to do with the way the speaker speaks. If it's Martin Weiss that I'm writing for, there are certain phrases I'm not going to use. But the way I see it is if this guy speaking in his voice could sell this product, why are they paying me? The bottom line is that these people, when they write, they're typically writing editorial copy at the very best, if that, or none at all, like the head of a vitamin company. So I don't worry about voice. Now people will come to me and say, this doesn't sound like me. And I'll say, well, OK, tell me what you object to, and I'll change it. But one of the things that I learned early on is the more things like that you hold in your head while you're trying to write, the more impossible it becomes to write. Well, you need to be able to do it. We're going to talk about mastering the art of the brain dump, not really caring about stuff like that. Now if you get to a final draft and you're loving it, but there's a phrase here or there that the client doesn't like, it doesn't sound like me, change it. But that would be my advice. Your goal is to create readership and sell a product. And it would be totally lost on the prospect as to whose voice you're writing in anyway. I did have one package where it was important to me. And it was the Dolan's. This husband-wife team who were financial advisors. And they were everywhere. They were on TV. They were in Forbes. They were in Fortune. They had a thing in USA Today or whatever. They were everywhere. And so now I got two problems. I got voices that people are familiar with. And I've got two spokespeople in the package. And so I wrote it like a transcript of a video. So I'd have Ken going on about this and the other thing. This is Daria. And I think Ken's full of it. And I'd have her say something. Because this is their personalities. And so on that, it was important because they were well known. And I had to work out a way to have two voices at once. So it was actually really fun to write that. And it worked great. Yeah. Break time. I just want to say something just full transparency. In these things, we tend to talk about all of our most brilliant work, all of our most brilliant successes. I got to tell you something. Especially in the early years, there were far more flops than there were great successes. And we're going to talk about why that doesn't have to cost your client tomorrow. But I just want to reiterate what Carline said. Don't let the disappointment of a promotion that doesn't work well for you. Slow you down because it happens to everybody. It happens to every one of us. And it still happens to us today. All right. Break. We'll be back at 15 till.
